name: Ruta del Cuyargayos — circular desde Soto de Agues
region_intro: Hiking around Sobrescobio offers access to a diverse landscape within the Redes Natural Park, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. The region features glacial valleys, jagged peaks, and extensive forests of beech and oak. Its topography is shaped by limestone, slate, and sandstone, with the Nalón River and its tributaries flowing through the area. Elevations range from river valleys to peaks like El Retriñón at 1,862 meters. Best hiking trails around Sobrescobio The most popular hiking route is Ruta del Cuyargayos — circular desde Soto de Agues, a 9.0 miles (14.5 km) difficult trail that takes 5 hours 16 minutes to complete. This route explores the natural environment around Soto de Agues, a village known for its traditional architecture. Another top favourite among local hikers is Retorturiu Stone Building – Soto de Agues Village loop from Sobrescobio, a moderate 7.2 miles (11.7 km) path. This trail offers views of traditional stone buildings and the village of Soto de Agues. Local hikers also love the La Xamoca Peak loop from Oviñana, a 9.0 miles (14.5 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 5 hours 28 minutes. This route provides ascents to higher elevations with panoramic views. Hiking around Sobrescobio is defined by river valleys, dense beech and oak forests, and limestone peaks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from comfortable paths to routes requiring good fitness. The routes in Sobrescobio are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 140 reviews. More than 1400 hikers have used komoot to explore Sobrescobio's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when hiking in Sobrescobio?
A: Sobrescobio, located within the Redes Natural Park, offers a diverse landscape characterized by glacial valleys, jagged peaks, and extensive forests of beech and oak. The topography is shaped by limestone, slate, and sandstone, with elevations ranging from river valleys to peaks like El Retriñón at 1,862 meters. You'll find a mix of comfortable paths and routes requiring good fitness and sure-footedness.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly hiking options in Sobrescobio?
A: Yes, Sobrescobio offers a variety of routes suitable for different ability levels, including comfortable paths ideal for families. While specific 'easy' routes aren't detailed in the top recommendations, the region is known for trails that cater to less experienced hikers. For example, the famous Ruta del Alba, starting from Soto de Agues, is generally considered easily hikeable and features spectacular natural surroundings.

Q: Can I find trails with waterfalls or impressive natural features in Sobrescobio?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in natural beauty. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5085055" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruta del Alba&lt;/a&gt; is particularly known for its cascades and waterfalls as it follows the Alba River through a narrowing gorge. Another spectacular natural feature is the Tabayón del Mongayu, a waterfall over 60 meters high, which is one of the natural monuments within the Redes Natural Park and accessible via various routes.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available in Sobrescobio?
A: Yes, many routes in Sobrescobio are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1047543542/ruta-del-cuyargayos-circular-desde-soto-de-agues" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruta del Cuyargayos — circular desde Soto de Agues&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 9.0-mile (14.5 km) trail exploring the natural environment around Soto de Agues. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14990252" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Retorturiu Stone Building – Soto de Agues Village loop from Sobrescobio&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 7.2-mile (11.7 km) path offering views of traditional architecture.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or points of interest to see while hiking?
A: Sobrescobio is home to several significant landmarks. The village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5554225" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soto de Agues&lt;/a&gt; is considered one of Asturias' most beautiful and serves as a gateway to popular routes. You can also visit the &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sobrescobio" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Deboyu Cave&lt;/a&gt;, a karstic cavity near Les Yanes, or the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5097008" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojo de Buey (L'Arcu de la Finiesta)&lt;/a&gt;, a natural arch in the Peña Mea massif. For panoramic views, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5151883" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coto Bello Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; is highly recommended.

Q: Is Sobrescobio a good destination for hiking with dogs?
A: Sobrescobio, as part of the Redes Natural Park and a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, offers many natural trails that are generally suitable for dogs. However, it's always advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in protected areas, to respect wildlife and other hikers. Check specific trail regulations before you go, as some areas might have restrictions during certain seasons or for conservation purposes.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Sobrescobio?
A: The region is beautiful year-round, but autumn is particularly captivating when the extensive beech and oak forests transform into an explosion of warm colors. Spring also offers lush greenery and pleasant temperatures. Summer is popular but can be warmer, while winter hiking is possible, especially on lower elevation trails, though higher routes may require appropriate gear for snow and ice.

Q: How accessible are the hiking trails by public transport or car parking?
A: While specific public transport details vary, many popular trailheads, such as those near Soto de Agues or Caleao, are typically accessible by car, and parking is often available in or near these villages. It's recommended to check local transport schedules and parking availability for your chosen starting point, especially during peak seasons, as information can change.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Sobrescobio?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 140 reviews. Reviewers often praise the stunning natural beauty, the variety of landscapes from river valleys to mountain peaks, and the well-maintained trails. The peaceful atmosphere and the chance to explore traditional Asturian villages like Soto de Agues are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there opportunities for bird watching in Sobrescobio?
A: Yes, the reservoirs of Tanes and Rioseco in Sobrescobio are excellent spots for bird watching. The region's status as a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve means it supports a rich diversity of birdlife. Purpose-built observation posts are available at these reservoirs, making it easier to spot various species.

Q: Can I find challenging hikes in Sobrescobio?
A: Yes, Sobrescobio offers several challenging routes for experienced hikers. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40310669" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Xamoca Peak loop from Oviñana&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 9.0-mile (14.5 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 5 hours 28 minutes, providing ascents to higher elevations with panoramic views. Other difficult routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1047543542/ruta-del-cuyargayos-circular-desde-soto-de-agues" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruta del Cuyargayos — circular desde Soto de Agues&lt;/a&gt; also offer significant elevation gain.
tips: Soto de Agues is a town in the council of Sobrescobio, located at 430 m altitude, at the end of one of the branches of the SC-2 road. It is divided into three neighborhoods: San Andrés, Agues and Soto. It preserves good examples of traditional architecture, a church dating from 1805, dedicated to San Andrés, and two hermitages, dedicated to San Antonio and Santa Ana, respectively. It is the starting point of several mountain routes, such as those of the Ruta del Alba (P. R. AS-62) and Cullargayos (P. R. AS-63).
A nice circular through the Redes area. You will need about two and a half hours to go up and about an hour and a half to go down.

It has a 17th century church and several old mansions and palaces dating from the 18th and 19th centuries. It is also known for being one of the few towns in Asturias that still speaks the Asturian language, which is a Romance language spoken in Asturias and some parts of the neighboring province of León.
